perturbation.original_text: Upon termination of any relationship betwe en the parties, Recipient will promptly deliver to the Soc iety, without retaining any copies, all documents a nd other materials furnished to Recipient by the Society.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon termination of any relationship between the parties, Recipient should deliver to the Society documents and other materials furnished to Recipient by the Society, without retaining any unnecessary copies.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'will promptly deliver' with 'should deliver' introduces ambiguity about the urgency of the return. The addition of 'unnecessary' before copies makes it subjective what constitutes a copy that needs to be delivered, undermining the mandatory return requirement.
perturbation.law_explanation: While not directly a privacy law, retaining copies of documents could infringe copyright if the Society owns the copyright and the recipient does not have permission to retain copies. The original text is clear and compliant, but the modified version by allowing to hold 'necessary copies' introduces doubt and ambiguity regarding copyright, thus the modified version might imply the breach of s 130A.

perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates the recipient 'will promptly deliver' all documents, creating a clear obligation. The modified text weakens this to 'should deliver,' introducing ambiguity about the obligation's strength. Further, the addition of 'unnecessary copies' introduces subjectivity, undermining the original intention of returning all documents. While the scraped snippets don't explicitly define copyright infringement as it relates to document retention, the Law Explanation suggests that retaining copies without permission could be problematic under copyright law. The change creates an Ambiguous Legal Obligation, by making it unclear what copies are necessary and what timeframe is considered acceptable for returning those copies, potentially leading to non-compliance, and therefore contradicting the original clear directive and creating a legal contradiction under the definition.

perturbation.original_text: A breach of any of the promises or agreements co ntained herein will result in irreparable and continuing damage to the Society fo r which there will be no adequate remedy at law, an d the Society shall be entitled to injunctive relief and/ or a decree for specific performance, and such othe r relief as may be proper (including monetary damages if approp riate).
perturbation.changed_text: A breach of any of the promises or agreements contained herein may result in damage to the Society, and the Society may be entitled to relief as deemed appropriate by the court.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'will result in irreparable and continuing damage' to 'may result in damage' and 'shall be entitled to injunctive relief' to 'may be entitled to relief as deemed appropriate by the court' significantly weakens the Society's legal recourse. It makes the potential damages uncertain and gives the court broad discretion, undermining the enforceability of the agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Contract Law Principles relating to Remedies for Breach of Contract
perturbation.law_explanation: Under Australian contract law, remedies for breach of contract should aim to put the injured party in the position they would have been in had the contract been performed. This modification weakens the legal certainty regarding remedies, potentially conflicting with the principle of fair compensation for breach of contract. The ambiguous wording may impede the Society's ability to claim specific performance or injunctive relief, remedies typically available for breaches of confidentiality agreements.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: contract | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you contract A contract is an agreement between parties , creating mutual obligations that are enforceable by law . The basic elements required for the agreement to be a legally enforceable contract are: mutual assent , expressed by a valid offer and acceptance ; adequate consideration ; capacity ; and legality . In some states , elements of consideration can be satisfied by a valid substitute. Possible remedies for breach of contract include general damages , consequential damages , reliance damages , and specific performance . Background : Contracts are promises that the law will enforce. Contract law is generally governed by state common law , and while general overall contract law is common throughout the country, some specific court interpretations of a particular element of the contract may vary between the states. If a promise is breached, the law provides remedies to the harmed party, often in the form of monetary damages, or in limited circumstances, in the form of specific performance of the promise made. Elements -- Consideration and Mutual Assent Contracts arise when a duty comes into existence, because of a promise made by one of the parties. To be legally binding as a contract, a promise must be exchanged for adequate consideration. There are two different theories or definitions of consideration: Bargain Theory of Consideration and Benefit-Detriment theory of consideration. Benefit-Detriment Under the b enefit-detriment theory, an adequate consideration exists only when a promise made to the benefit of the promisor or to the detriment of the promisee, which reasonably and fairly induces the promisor to make a promise for something else for the promisee. For example, promises that are purely gifts are not considered enforceable because the personal satisfaction the grantor of the promise may receive from the act of generosity is normally not considered sufficient detriment to constitute adequate consideration. Bargain-for-Exchange Under Bargain-for-Exchange theory of consideration, adequate consideration exists when a promisor makes a promise in return for something else. Here, the essential condition is that the promisor was given something specifically to induce the promise being made. In other words, the bargain for exchange theory is different from the detriment-benefit theory in that the focus in bargain for exchange theory seems to be the parties’ motive for making the promises and the parties’ subjective mutual assent, while in detriment benefit theory, the focus seems to be an objective legal detriment or benefit to the parties. Governing Laws Contracts are mainly governed by state statutory and common (judge-made) law and private law (i.e. the private agreement). Private law principally includes the terms of the agreement between the parties who are exchanging promises. This private law may override many of the rules otherwise established by state law. Statutory law, such as the Statute of Fraud , may require some kinds of contracts be put in writing and executed with particular formalities, for the contract to be enforceable. Otherwise, the parties may enter into a binding agreement without signing a formal written document. For example, Virginia Supreme Court has held in Lucy v. Zehmer that even an agreement made on a piece of napkin can be considered a valid contract, if the parties were both sane, and showed mutual assent and consideration. Most of the principles of the common law of contracts are outlined in the Restatement of Law, Second Contracts published by the American Law Institute. The Uniform Commercial Code , whose original articles have been adopted in nearly every state, represents a body of statutory law that governs important categories of contracts. The main articles that deal with the law of contracts are Article 1 (General Provisions) and Article 2 (Sales) . Sections of Article 9 (
perturbation.justification: The modification significantly weakens the Society's legal recourse by changing 'will result in irreparable and continuing damage' to 'may result in damage' and 'shall be entitled to injunctive relief' to 'may be entitled to relief as deemed appropriate by the court'. This creates ambiguity and undermines the enforceability of the agreement. The original text clearly defines the consequences of a breach and the Society's rights, while the changed text makes both uncertain. According to Scraped Law Snippet 1, contracts create 'mutual obligations that are enforceable by law' and if a promise is breached, 'the law provides remedies to the harmed party'. The changed text weakens the enforceability and remedy aspects of the contract for the Society, creating a contradiction, as it moves away from ensuring remedies towards potentially discretionary relief decided by the court.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement may only be changed by mutual agreement between the parties in writing.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement may be changed through reasonable communication between the parties.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirement for a 'written' agreement and replacing it with 'reasonable communication' introduces ambiguity. It's unclear what constitutes 'reasonable communication,' making it difficult to prove whether a change was validly agreed upon. This could allow for unilateral changes or disputes over the terms of the agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Statute of Frauds (various state implementations based on English law)
perturbation.law_citation: Various state implementations of the Statute of Frauds
perturbation.law_explanation: While Australia does not have a direct Statute of Frauds in the same way as some US states, the principle that certain agreements (especially those involving significant rights or obligations) should be in writing to be enforceable is widely accepted. Removing the 'in writing' requirement and replacing it with 'reasonable communication' may make it difficult to prove the existence and terms of any changes to the agreement, rendering them unenforceable. This ambiguity can undermine the integrity and enforceability of the contract.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: statute of frauds | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you statute of frauds Statute of frauds is a statute requiring certain contracts to be in writing and signed by the parties bound by the contract. The purpose is to prevent fraud and other injury. The most common types of contracts to which the statute applies are contracts that involve the sale or transfer of land, (see: real estate transactions ), and contracts that cannot be completed within one year. See also: Y amaha Motor Corp., U.S.A. v. Calhoun , 516 U.S. 199 (1996) Contracts for the sale of goods worth $500 or more. UCC § 2-201 [Last reviewed in June of 2024 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE commercial activities finance financial services housing PROPERTY landlord & tenant property & real estate law business law contracts wex definitions business sectors commercial transactions property law statutes Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The change from requiring modifications to be 'in writing' to allowing them through 'reasonable communication' directly contradicts the principle outlined in the scraped law snippet from Wex, which states that the statute of frauds requires certain contracts to be in writing and signed to prevent fraud. While the snippet refers to specific types of contracts (land, sales over $500), the underlying principle that significant agreements should be in writing for enforceability is undermined by the modified text. The ambiguity introduced by 'reasonable communication' makes it difficult to prove the existence and terms of any changes, weakening the contract's enforceability, which creates a significant issue from a legal perspective and fails to uphold the principle of preventing fraud.
